speaker: interviewer
text: It's June 29, 1995. Mrs. Balfour, we were just talking about your having not discussed your experiences during the war with your daughter. So you feel that whatever you went through had a direct influence on you in the way you raised your daughter and the way you handled her growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it had-- it had a direct influence on everything I did in my life. As I said-- as I said, it castrated certain-- I don't know, say certain sensory parts. There are feelings I never had and I never will have, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't want to transmit this kind of thing to her. I thought that the, you know-- the more normal she was brought up and the more normal she thought the world-- the world was, the better for her. I don't know, maybe I was wrong, but I still think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the war, after you were already in Argentina, did you have contact with Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, he came to Argentina with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us what your recollections of him after the war were, what you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, he was a good looking man. He-- he liked women. He liked his vodka. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was very cheerful and, funnily enough, very unsuccessful at whatever he tried to do. You would think a man like that would-- would be successful. Well, he tried to-- once to raise-- not minks. They're animals that are similar to minks. Nutrias. They all died off. Then he tried to raise chicken. Wasn't any more successful at that than-- than at the nutrias. He was-- he was a man who liked to live well. Not terribly intelligent, a nice guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously you knew him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew him personally for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the point in time where you were already educated and you were already older and having gone through everything you went through, were you ever able to intellectually discuss with him your feelings about what he did, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never talked about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never discussed it with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never asked him why he did what he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you suppose that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, it was part of the-- or my thing. I never talked to anyone about any of it, not my parents, not their friends, not-- not-- not anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you realize that he had actually saved your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was told. I mean, I realized because I was told afterwards. But somehow, at that point, didn't matter anymore. It just-- it was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your parents ever thanked him or discussed it with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess. But they also liked talking about all this and I don't think he did very much, either. He'd rather go out dancing somewhere or-- he was one of my adult aunt's boyfriend for a very, very many years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He didn't live with his wife ever. I mean, he-- she was over there. Emily was there but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she in Argentina with him at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure. But she lived on the farm and he had an apartment in-- in town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there wasn't much-- oh, we used to go out to the farm, occasionally, all of us to see the latest-- the latest effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was his wife towards you? How did she act towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way she acts towards the whole-- the whole world. She's very peculiar. She always was. She's very, very closed, doesn't say much. She's very old now, of course, she's about 90-- more than 90. No, the only thing I resent is that she's been-- when-- when was-- was first-- first came to the movies, was first released, an awful lot of reporters-- she was called to all-- to all kinds of shows, et cetera, et cetera. And I resented very much the fact that she kept on saying that Schindler-- that Oskar-- didn't do any-- any of those things. She was the one who did them, which is quite untrue. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why he did these things. I really don't, because he wasn't at all of the type of savior, you know? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know Muhammad, Jesus Christ, whatever-- not at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. I-- I-- I thought about it sometimes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't really think it was out of the-- simply out of the goodness of his heart. He must have had fairly good reasons for doing it. Now, what the reasons are-- I'm grateful for-- for-- for-- to him for having saved me, but it's sort of a little bit tongue in cheek gratitude because I really would like have-- to have known what his reasons were for doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, imagine, somebody who had the type of character that he had. He needed all the good things in life. The-- the type of man who must have, not riches, but oh, caviar, and champagne, and beautiful women, and the well liver. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Usually, they're not philanthropists. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] However, I never asked, so I never found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very much for coming here today to give us your testimony. I know that you're here visiting from Buenos Aires and that you're going back soon. And we'd like to thank you on behalf of the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation for giving us your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to see some of your pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing up, from left to right, is my mother, Roma Horowitz, my father, [? Mundic ?] Horowitz, a cousin of my father's, [? Muncie ?] [? Roesner, ?] and sitting down, from left to right again, that's me and my cousin, [? Oleg ?] [? Roesner. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Munich, in a part of Munich called [? Lime. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946. From-- again, from left to right, my mother, Roma Horowitz, myself, my father, [? Mundic ?] Horowitz, or Edmund Horowitz. And this also has been taken 1946 in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And give us your name again, so that anyone who's watching will know.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Halina Horowitz. Well, this is me, Halina Horowitz-Balfour, Munich, 1946 again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were approximately 12 or 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not approximately. I was 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: 13 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Left to right, [? Muncie ?] [? Roesner, ?] my father's cousin, myself, Oskar Schindler, a lady whose name I don't remember, my father, my mother, and I don't remember the-- the names of the rest of the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father and mother were named?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edmund Horowitz and Roma Horowitz-- and Roma Horowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: From left to right, [? Muncie ?] [? Roesner, ?] a cousin of my father's, my father, Edmund Horowitz, [? Milla ?] [? Page, ?] a friend of my parents, Oskar Schindler, [INAUDIBLE] [? Springer, ?] another friend. And sitting down, myself, Halina Balfour, [? Oleg ?] [? Roesner, ?] a cousin of mine, and [? Pescha-- ?] I don't remember her surname. It's on the back there, but I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also taking in '46, in Munchen, [? Lime. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is Noel Jacqueline Balfour, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this has been taken in Los Angeles in 1994.
